analogy: An API is like a waiter in a restaurant. You don't go into the kitchen yourself — the waiter takes your order, brings it to the kitchen, and returns with your food. Similarly, an API takes your request to a system and brings back the response, without you needing to know how the kitchen works.
An API is like a recipe card that only lists what ingredients you need to provide and what dish you'll get back. You don't need to know the chef's techniques — just follow the interface.

An API is like the scoreboard at a stadium. It gives you the information you need (score, time, stats) without requiring you to understand how the timing systems, sensors, and databases behind it work.

Recursion is like standing between two mirrors facing each other. Each mirror reflects the other, creating an infinite series of reflections — each one a smaller version of the same image. Recursion works the same way: a function calls itself, each time with a slightly smaller problem, until it hits a base case (like the mirrors getting too small to see).

Recursion is like a recipe that says 'to make lasagna, make a smaller lasagna and put another layer on top.' You keep making smaller lasagnas until you reach the base case: a single layer. Then you build back up.

Recursion is like a fern leaf. If you look at a single branch of a fern, it looks like a miniature version of the whole fern. Each sub-branch repeats the same pattern at a smaller scale — that's recursion in nature.

Recursion is like a tournament bracket. To find the champion, you need the winner of two semi-finals. To find each semi-final winner, you need the winners of quarter-finals. You keep breaking it down until you reach individual matches — the base case.

A cache is like keeping your most-used kitchen tools on the counter instead of in a deep drawer. It takes longer to dig through the drawer, so you keep the things you reach for most often within arm's reach.

A cache is like a mise en place — pre-measured, pre-chopped ingredients laid out before cooking. Instead of going back to the fridge and cutting board every time, everything you need is already prepared and at hand.

A cache is like a quarterback's pre-snap read. Instead of analyzing every defender after the snap, they memorize key formations beforehand. When they see a familiar setup, they already know where to throw — no need to recalculate.
Encryption is like sending a letter in a locked box. Anyone can see the box being delivered, but only the person with the right key can open it and read the letter inside.
Encryption is like writing a song in a made-up language. Anyone can hear the melody, but only someone who knows the language can understand the lyrics. The 'key' is knowledge of that language.
Encryption is like a secret family recipe where the ingredients are written in code. 'Add 3 cups of sunshine' might mean '3 cups of lemon juice.' Without the decoder, no one can replicate the dish.

Load balancing is like a grocery store opening new checkout lanes when lines get long. Instead of everyone waiting in one lane, customers are distributed across multiple lanes so everyone gets served faster.

Load balancing is like a basketball coach distributing minutes across players. If one player plays the entire game, they'll burn out. By rotating players in and out, the team maintains performance throughout the game.

Load balancing is like a head chef assigning dishes across multiple line cooks. If one cook gets all the orders, the kitchen slows down. By distributing orders evenly, food comes out faster and more reliably.

Version control is like Google Docs' version history, but much more powerful. You can see every change ever made, who made it, go back to any previous version, and even create parallel 'what if' versions of the document without affecting the original.

Version control is like keeping every revision of architectural blueprints. If a new design causes structural problems, you can pull out the previous revision and see exactly what changed. You can even have two teams working on different blueprint variations simultaneously.

Training a machine learning model is like learning to cook by taste-testing. You make a dish, taste it (evaluate), adjust the seasoning (update parameters), and try again. Over hundreds of iterations, you learn what combination of ingredients produces the best result — without anyone giving you the exact recipe.

Training a model is like a basketball player practicing free throws. Each shot gives feedback — too far left, too short. Over thousands of shots, they adjust their form until they consistently score. The player doesn't memorize each exact shot; they develop an intuition (a model) for the right motion.

Training a model is like learning to play guitar by ear. You hear a note, try to match it, hear the difference, and adjust your fingers. Over time, you develop the ability to play songs you've never heard before — you've learned the patterns, not just memorized specific songs.

A TCP handshake is like a phone call. You dial (SYN), the other person says 'Hello?' (SYN-ACK), and you say 'Hi, it's me' (ACK). Only after this three-step exchange do you start the actual conversation. If any step fails, you hang up and try again.

A TCP handshake is like the pre-game coin toss ritual. The referee flips the coin (SYN), one captain calls it (SYN-ACK), and the referee confirms the result (ACK). Only after this agreed-upon exchange does the game begin.

DNS is like the contacts app on your phone. You don't memorize your friend's phone number (192.168.1.1) — you just tap their name (google.com) and your phone looks up the number for you. DNS does the same thing for the internet.

DNS is like a building directory in a lobby. You don't need to know that Acme Corp is on floor 14, suite 1402. You look up the company name in the directory and it tells you where to go. DNS translates human-friendly website names into the actual server addresses.

Git branching is like a tree growing new branches. The trunk is your main code. When you want to try something new, you grow a branch off to the side. If the branch bears fruit (the feature works), you graft it back onto the trunk. If not, you prune it — the trunk is unaffected.

Git branching is like splitting a batch of soup into two pots. You experiment with new spices in one pot while keeping the original untouched. If the experiment tastes great, you use that recipe going forward. If not, you still have the original.

Overfitting is like a tennis player who only practices against one opponent. They learn every quirk and habit of that opponent and dominate them — but lose to everyone else. They memorized one opponent instead of learning general tennis skills.

Overfitting is like memorizing one specific recipe so precisely that you measure ingredients to the milligram — but you can't improvise or adapt when an ingredient is missing. You learned the recipe, not how to cook.
Overfitting is like a musician who can play one song perfectly note-for-note but can't jam or improvise. They memorized the recording instead of learning the underlying music theory.

Middleware is like airport security between the check-in desk and the gate. Every passenger (request) has to pass through it. Security doesn't handle flights or check-ins — it just inspects, validates, and either lets you through or stops you. Middleware sits between the request and your application, doing similar inspection and processing.

Middleware is like a food taster in a royal kitchen. Every dish passes through the taster between the chef and the king. The taster doesn't cook or eat the meal — they just check it and pass it along (or reject it). Middleware inspects and processes requests before they reach your main application.

Rate limiting is like a bouncer at a club who only lets in 10 people per minute. Even if 100 people show up at once, the bouncer controls the flow to prevent overcrowding. If you've already entered too many times tonight, you're told to come back later.

Rate limiting is like a dam on a river. The river might surge after heavy rain, but the dam controls how much water flows downstream at any given time, preventing floods. Rate limiting does the same for server requests.

The internet is like a postal system for data. Your message gets split into postcards (packets), each stamped with the destination address. They may travel different routes through sorting offices (routers), but they all arrive and get reassembled into the original letter at the other end.

Public-key encryption is like a mailbox on the street. Anyone can drop a letter in through the slot (encrypt with the public key), but only the person with the mailbox key can open it and read the letter (decrypt with the private key).

Lossy compression is like summarizing a novel into a short synopsis. You keep the essential plot but lose the vivid descriptions and minor subplots. You can't reconstruct the original book from the summary, but for most purposes the synopsis tells you what you need to know.

File compression is like vacuum-sealing clothes for travel. The clothes are still all there — you haven't thrown anything away — they just take up less space in your suitcase. When you unzip, everything puffs back to its original size.

Sending a folder as a ZIP file is like packing many small boxes into one large shipping container. It's easier to ship one container than track dozens of individual packages, and the post office handles one delivery instead of many.

Upscaling a small image is like trying to enlarge a mosaic by adding tiles. You can guess what colors should fill the gaps, but you're inventing detail that was never there. The result may look plausible but it's the computer's best guess, not the real picture.

Downscaling an image is like looking at a city from an airplane. You can still see the general layout — parks, rivers, major roads — but individual buildings and cars are too small to distinguish. The information is simplified, not distorted.

Pixel images are like mosaic tile art — zoom in and you see individual squares. Vector images are like stencils — they're mathematical shapes that stay crisp no matter how large you draw them, because you're scaling the instructions, not the dots.

Download and upload speeds are like a highway with unequal lanes. Your ISP builds six lanes coming toward your house (download) but only one lane going out (upload), because most people consume far more content than they send.

A DDoS attack is like thousands of people calling the same pizza shop at once. The phone lines are jammed, so real customers can't get through. Nobody's breaking in — they're just overwhelming the capacity to serve legitimate orders.

Net neutrality is like a highway where all cars travel at the same speed limit regardless of who manufactured them. Without it, the highway owner could create a fast lane for certain brands and slow everyone else down unless they pay extra.

ISP peering disputes are like two shipping companies arguing over who pays to maintain the bridge connecting their territories. When they disagree, trucks (your data) get stuck in traffic at the border crossing.

A network backdoor is like a secret service entrance to a building that bypasses all security checks. Useful for maintenance, dangerous if someone unauthorized finds it — because they walk right in without anyone noticing.

The OS manages RAM like a librarian manages desk space. When a student (program) sits down, the librarian assigns them a section. When they leave, that section is freed up. The librarian tracks who's sitting where so no one overwrites another student's work.

concept: RAM management (free RAM is wasted RAM)
analogy: Free RAM is wasted RAM, like empty tables in a restaurant during rush hour. The system keeps recently used data in RAM (seats diners at open tables) because it's faster than fetching from disk (making them wait in line outside). Truly free RAM isn't helping anyone.

Overclocking is like running a car engine at higher RPMs than the manufacturer recommends. You get more speed, but the engine runs hotter, wears out faster, and might break down if you push too hard without better cooling.

Computer overheating is like running a marathon in a winter coat. The processor generates heat with every calculation, and if the cooling system can't carry that heat away fast enough, components slow themselves down (thermal throttling) or shut off to avoid permanent damage.

Dividing by zero is like asking 'how many times does nothing fit into something?' The question has no meaningful answer, so the computer throws an error rather than guessing — like a calculator displaying 'ERROR' because the math itself is broken.

Logarithmic time is like finding a word in a dictionary. You don't read every page — you open to the middle, see if your word is before or after, and repeat. Each step cuts the remaining pages in half, so even a million-page dictionary only takes about 20 steps.

A machine learning algorithm is like a student who learns by doing thousands of practice exams. They never see the actual test, but by recognizing patterns in practice questions, they can make educated guesses on new problems they've never encountered.

Programming in assembly is like giving someone driving directions one tiny step at a time: 'Turn the steering wheel 15 degrees left. Press the gas pedal 2 centimeters. Wait 3 seconds.' It's precise and fast, but exhausting compared to just saying 'turn left at the light.'

C# versions are like annual editions of a cookbook. Each new edition adds new recipes and better techniques, but all your old recipes still work. You don't need to throw away your old dishes — you just get more options in the newer book.

An LCD screen works like thousands of tiny window blinds. Behind them is a constant backlight. Each blind (pixel) can twist to let through more or less light, and color filters in front tint that light red, green, or blue. Together, they form a picture.

An E-ink screen is like an Etch A Sketch — it holds the image with no power, but to change the picture, it has to shake all the tiny particles and rearrange them. That shake is the flicker you see on every page turn.

Wi-Fi signals travel through walls like sound travels through a closed door — you can still hear it, but it's muffled. Each wall absorbs some signal. Thick concrete is like a soundproof room; thin drywall barely dampens it at all.

Your phone showing Wi-Fi briefly after the router dies is like a GPS still showing your position for a few seconds after entering a tunnel. The device is displaying its last known state and hasn't yet realized the connection is gone.

Wireless data transfer is like a very fast flickering flashlight sending Morse code. The transmitter rapidly switches radio waves on and off in patterns, and the receiver decodes those patterns back into data — just at billions of flashes per second.

A flash drive is like a sticky note — small, portable, stores a quick message. An external hard drive is like a filing cabinet — bulkier, but holds vastly more documents. Flash drives have no moving parts (instant access); hard drives have spinning platters (slightly slower but much more storage per dollar).

Increasing hard drive density each year is like writing smaller and smaller handwriting on the same size paper. Better tools (finer pens, magnifying glasses) let you fit more words per page without needing a bigger sheet.

Digital audio output is like translating sheet music into sound. The digital file contains precise instructions (samples) describing the sound wave at thousands of points per second. The DAC (digital-to-analog converter) reads these instructions and recreates the original wave for your speakers.

concept: Inertial reference frames (jumping in a plane)
analogy: You land in the same spot when jumping in a plane because you, the air, and the plane are all moving at 500 mph together. Jumping adds a little upward speed but doesn't change your forward speed. It's like jumping inside a smoothly moving train — you share its velocity.

Docker containers are like food trucks. Each truck has everything it needs inside — kitchen, ingredients, equipment — and can set up anywhere. It doesn't matter if the parking lot runs Windows or Linux; the truck works the same way. That's containerization: packaging an app with all its dependencies so it runs identically everywhere.

A database index is like the index at the back of a textbook. Without it, finding every mention of 'photosynthesis' means reading every page. With it, you jump straight to pages 47, 103, and 256. The tradeoff: the index takes up space and needs updating whenever you add content.

Programmers think by decomposing problems — like a chef breaking a complex dish into prep steps. They identify patterns ('this is similar to a problem I've solved'), build abstractions ('I'll make a reusable sauce base'), and work iteratively ('taste and adjust'). The skill isn't typing code — it's thinking in systems.

Tokens are how an AI reads text — not word by word, but in chunks, like how you might read 'un-believe-able' as three pieces. Common words are one token, rare words get split into fragments. The AI doesn't see letters or words — it sees a sequence of these puzzle pieces.

A context window is the AI's short-term memory — like a whiteboard with limited space. Everything you've said in the conversation is written on it. Once the board is full, the oldest notes get erased to make room. A bigger context window means a bigger whiteboard.

Temperature controls how creative vs predictable the AI is. Low temperature is like asking a chef to make their signature dish — you get the most likely, reliable answer. High temperature is like saying 'surprise me' — the chef experiments, sometimes brilliantly, sometimes oddly.

Prompt engineering is like giving directions to a very capable but very literal assistant. 'Write me something about dogs' gets a generic result. 'Write a 200-word vet's guide to puppy nutrition for first-time owners' gets exactly what you need. The skill is in how precisely you ask.

AI hallucination is like a confident student who never says 'I don't know.' When the AI doesn't have the answer, it doesn't stay silent — it generates plausible-sounding text that follows the pattern of real answers. It's not lying; it's a pattern-completion machine that has no concept of truth.

RAG is like giving a student an open-book exam instead of asking them to answer from memory. Before the AI responds, it searches a knowledge base for relevant documents and includes them in its context. The AI still writes the answer, but now it has reference material to draw from.

Embeddings convert words into coordinates on a map. Similar concepts end up near each other — 'king' and 'queen' are close, 'king' and 'banana' are far apart. This lets computers measure meaning mathematically, like measuring distance on a map instead of arguing about what words mean.

Prompting is like giving a skilled chef verbal instructions for tonight's dish. Fine-tuning is like sending that chef to culinary school for a specific cuisine — it permanently changes how they cook. Prompting is quick and flexible; fine-tuning is expensive but deeply changes the model's behavior.

Streaming an AI response is like watching someone write on a whiteboard in real time versus waiting for them to finish and then revealing the board. The total time is similar, but streaming feels faster because you start reading immediately instead of staring at a blank screen.

AI guardrails are like bumpers in a bowling lane. They don't change how you throw the ball, but they keep it from going into the gutter. Safety filters check the AI's input and output against rules, blocking or modifying responses that cross predefined boundaries.

Frontend is everything the customer sees in a restaurant — the menu, the decor, the plating. Backend is the kitchen — where the actual cooking happens, invisible to diners. A beautiful frontend with a broken backend is a gorgeous restaurant with no chef.

An HTTP request is like ordering at a counter. You walk up (connect), say what you want (request), and the server either hands you your food (200 OK), says they're out (404 Not Found), or the kitchen catches fire (500 Server Error). Every webpage is the result of this exchange.

JSON is a universal language for data, like a standardized shipping label. No matter what's in the box, the label always follows the same format: name, address, weight. Computers that speak different programming languages can all read JSON, making it the common tongue of the web.

A REST API is like a set menu — there are fixed dishes (endpoints) and you get what's listed. GraphQL is like a build-your-own-bowl — you specify exactly which ingredients you want and get only those. REST is simpler; GraphQL is more flexible but requires more setup.

OAuth is like a hotel key card system. Instead of giving every app your master password (room key), you log in once with Google/GitHub (front desk), and they issue a limited key card that only opens specific doors. The app never sees your actual password.

Cookies are like a wristband at an amusement park. The first time you enter (log in), you get a wristband (cookie). On every ride after that, you just flash your wristband instead of buying a new ticket. The session is the park's record of which wristband belongs to which visitor.

CORS errors happen when your frontend tries to fetch data from a different server, like a student trying to borrow a book from another school's library. The other school's librarian (server) has a list of approved schools. If yours isn't on it, you get rejected — even if the book is publicly available.

Regular HTTP is like sending letters — you write, send, wait for a reply. WebSockets are like a phone call — once connected, both sides can talk freely at any time without re-dialing. That's why chat apps and live dashboards use WebSockets: they need instant, two-way communication.

A webhook is like asking a store to call you when your order is ready, instead of calling them every 5 minutes to check. Instead of your app constantly polling 'anything new?', the external service pushes a notification to your URL the moment something happens.

Environment variables are like the combination to a safe written on a sticky note inside the manager's office — not in the store's public handbook. API keys and passwords live in env variables so they never end up in your code, which might be shared publicly on GitHub.

Localhost is your computer pretending to be a server — like rehearsing a play in your living room before performing on stage. The port number (e.g., :3000) is like an apartment number in a building — it tells traffic which app to go to, since many apps can run on your machine simultaneously.

HTML is the skeleton of a webpage — the bones and structure. CSS is the skin and clothing — colors, fonts, layout. JavaScript is the muscles and nervous system — it makes things move, react, and change. You can have a page with just HTML (a skeleton), but it takes all three for a living, interactive site.

The DOM is the browser's live blueprint of your webpage. It takes your HTML file and builds a tree of objects in memory — like turning architectural plans into an actual LEGO model. JavaScript can rearrange the LEGO pieces (DOM nodes) without rewriting the original plans.

Local storage is like a locker at the gym — your stuff stays there until you clean it out, even if you leave and come back tomorrow. Session storage is like a coat check — your stuff is held only while you're at the event. Close the browser tab, and session storage is gone.

A URL with query parameters like `?sort=price&color=red` is like a library request slip. The base URL says which shelf (page) you want, and the parameters are your specific preferences — 'sorted by price, only red ones.' The server reads the slip and customizes what it sends back.

Responsive design is like water filling different-shaped containers. The same content rearranges itself to fit a phone screen, tablet, or desktop — stacking vertically on narrow screens, spreading horizontally on wide ones. Media queries are the rules that say 'if the container is this size, arrange like this.'

Server-side rendering is like getting a fully assembled IKEA shelf delivered. Client-side rendering is like getting the flat-pack — your browser (the customer) assembles it using JavaScript. SSR shows content faster initially; CSR can feel more interactive once it loads.

CRUD — Create, Read, Update, Delete — is the four things you can do with any piece of data, like the four things you can do with a contact in your phone: add a new one, look one up, change a number, or delete it. Almost every app is just CRUD wrapped in a nice interface.

HTTP status codes are like a waiter's short replies. 200 means 'here's your order.' 301 means 'that dish moved to a new menu, I'll bring you there.' 404 means 'we don't have that.' 500 means 'the kitchen is on fire.' The number tells your browser what happened without needing a full explanation.

Routing is like a building's directory in the lobby. When you visit `/about`, the router says 'that's the second floor.' When you visit `/products/shoes`, it says 'third floor, aisle 4.' It maps URL paths to the right page or function, so each address shows the correct content.

A package manager is like a grocery delivery service for code. Instead of growing your own tomatoes (writing every library from scratch), you order them from npm or pip. The package manager fetches the right version, handles dependencies ('this recipe also needs basil'), and keeps everything organized.

Dependency conflicts are like two recipes that each need a different version of the same ingredient — one needs fresh basil, the other needs dried. Your project can't have both, so you're stuck until you find a version that satisfies both recipes or swap one out.

CI/CD is like a factory assembly line with quality checkpoints. Every time you push code, the pipeline automatically runs tests (quality inspection), builds the app (assembly), and deploys it (ships to the store). If any checkpoint fails, the line stops before a defective product reaches customers.

Serverless is like a taxi vs owning a car. You don't maintain a server (car) that sits idle most of the day. Instead, a cloud provider spins up a function (taxi) only when a request comes in, runs your code, and shuts down. You pay per ride, not for the parked car.

An SSL certificate is like a notarized ID for a website. When you see the padlock in your browser, it means a trusted authority verified 'yes, this really is bankofamerica.com, not an imposter.' HTTPS then encrypts the conversation so no one can eavesdrop on the data in transit.

SQL databases are like spreadsheets — structured rows and columns, everything neatly organized, great for data with clear relationships. NoSQL databases are like filing cabinets of folders — each folder can contain different things, flexible and fast for unstructured or rapidly changing data.

A database migration is like renovating an occupied building. You can't demolish and rebuild — people live there (data exists). Instead, you carefully add a new room (column), move furniture (transform data), or knock out a wall (change schema) while keeping everything running.

Latency is how long one car takes to drive through a tunnel. Throughput is how many cars pass through per hour. A narrow tunnel has low throughput but could have low latency. A wide tunnel handles many cars (high throughput) but each car might still take the same time (same latency).

A CDN is like a franchise restaurant chain. Instead of everyone ordering from one central kitchen (origin server), copies of the food are pre-made at local branches worldwide. Customers get served from the nearest location, so delivery is faster and the central kitchen isn't overwhelmed.

Logging is like a security camera system for your app. You can't watch every room in real time, but when something goes wrong, you rewind the footage (logs) to see what happened. Observability adds dashboards — like a control room showing vital signs in real time, not just after the fact.

A commit is like a save point in a video game. It captures a snapshot of your entire project at that moment. If you mess up later, you can always load a previous save. The commit message is a note to your future self: 'saved right before I fought the boss.'

The staging area is like a packing table before you ship a box. You don't throw everything into the box at once — you place items on the table (stage files), check that you have the right things, then seal the box (commit). It lets you be selective about what goes into each save point.

A pull request is like submitting a draft for peer review. You've written your changes on a separate branch (your draft), and now you're asking the team to review it before it gets merged into the main document. Reviewers can comment, suggest edits, or approve it for publishing.

Git stash is like sweeping everything off your desk into a drawer when someone needs your workspace. Your work-in-progress is safely stored but out of sight. When you're ready, you pull it back out of the drawer and continue exactly where you left off.

A merge conflict is like two people editing the same paragraph of a document simultaneously. Git can auto-merge changes to different paragraphs, but when two edits touch the same lines, it stops and says 'I can't decide which version to keep — you pick.'

Technical debt is like taking a shortcut through a muddy field instead of walking around on the path. You arrive faster today, but now there's mud tracked everywhere that you'll eventually have to clean up. The longer you wait, the harder it dries and the more work it takes.

Refactoring is like reorganizing a messy closet. The clothes (functionality) don't change — you still own the same shirts and pants. But now everything is sorted, folded, and easy to find. The outside looks the same; the inside is dramatically easier to work with.

Open source licenses are like sharing a recipe. MIT license says 'use it however you want, just credit me.' GPL says 'you can use it, but if you modify and share it, your version must also be open.' Some let you commercialize freely; others require you to share back.

A CPU is like a brilliant professor who solves one complex problem at a time very quickly. A GPU is like a classroom of 1,000 students who can each solve a simple math problem simultaneously. AI workloads are millions of simple calculations, which is why GPUs dominate — they throw thousands of workers at the task in parallel.

CPU cores are like checkout lanes at a grocery store. More cores means more lanes open simultaneously. Threads are like each cashier handling two customers by alternating between them while one bags groceries. An 8-core, 16-thread CPU has 8 lanes, each juggling 2 customers.

Clock speed is the heartbeat of a processor — how many times per second it can tick through instructions. 3 GHz means 3 billion ticks per second. A faster clock doesn't always mean faster performance (a cheetah's heart beats fast, but an elephant carries more per step), but within the same chip design, higher is faster.

An HDD is like a record player — a needle reads data off a spinning platter, so it has to physically move to find information. An SSD is like a USB stick — no moving parts, just electronic chips. SSDs find data instantly (no waiting for the platter to spin around), which is why they feel so much faster.

VRAM is the GPU's personal desk space. When running a local AI model, the entire model needs to fit on this desk. If the model is too big for the VRAM, it's like trying to spread a king-size blueprint on a card table — it either won't fit or you have to keep folding and unfolding sections, which is painfully slow.

Bandwidth is the width of a highway — how many cars can travel side by side. Latency is the length of the highway — how long one car takes to reach the destination. A wide highway (high bandwidth) doesn't help if the road is 1,000 miles long (high latency). You need both to be good for a fast experience.

Computers think in binary (0s and 1s) because transistors are tiny switches — on or off. One switch is a bit. Eight switches together (a byte) can represent 256 different values — enough for one letter. A megabyte is a million of these groups, a gigabyte is a billion. It's all just switches at the bottom.

ARM and x86 are like two different languages that CPUs speak. x86 (Intel/AMD) has a huge vocabulary — powerful but complex and power-hungry. ARM (Apple Silicon, phones) uses a smaller, simpler vocabulary that runs cooler and sips battery. Software compiled for one can't run on the other without a translator (emulator).

Thermal throttling is your computer's self-preservation instinct. When the CPU gets too hot, it voluntarily slows down — like a runner slowing from a sprint to a jog to avoid heatstroke. Performance drops, but the chip survives. Better cooling lets it sprint longer before throttling kicks in.

Computer storage is a speed-vs-size pyramid. At the top: registers (a Post-it note on the CPU's desk — tiny but instant). Then cache (a drawer in the desk). Then RAM (a filing cabinet across the room). Then disk (a warehouse across town). Each level is bigger but slower to access, so the computer keeps its most-used data as close as possible.

Tokens are to an AI what syllables are to a reader sounding out a word. The AI breaks 'unbelievable' into chunks like 'un-believ-able' — common words are one chunk, unusual ones get split. The model's 'vocabulary' is these chunks, not whole words.

A context window is like a conversation on a phone with limited battery. Everything said so far drains the battery. Once it dies, the oldest parts of the conversation are forgotten. A larger battery (bigger context) lets you have a longer, more coherent discussion.

Temperature is like the dial on a creative writing workshop. Turn it to zero and every participant writes the most obvious, predictable sentence. Crank it up and they take wild creative risks — some brilliant, some nonsensical. The sweet spot depends on whether you need reliability or novelty.

Prompt engineering is like briefing a contractor. 'Build me something nice' gets unpredictable results. 'Build a 10x12 deck with cedar planks, railing on three sides, and two steps down to the yard' gets exactly what you want. The more precise the brief, the better the output.

An AI hallucinating is like autocomplete on your phone finishing a sentence with something grammatically correct but factually wrong. The model predicts what words should come next based on patterns, not based on checking whether the statement is true.

RAG is like a lawyer who researches case law before writing their argument. Instead of relying solely on what they remember from law school (training data), they pull relevant precedents (retrieved documents) and weave them into their response.

Embeddings are like GPS coordinates for meaning. Just as latitude and longitude place any location on a map, embeddings place any word or sentence in a mathematical space where distance equals similarity. 'Happy' and 'joyful' are neighbors; 'happy' and 'carburetor' are continents apart.

Prompting is like giving a general-purpose translator a phrase book for a specific meeting. Fine-tuning is like sending them to live in that country for a year. One is quick and temporary; the other permanently reshapes how they communicate.

Streaming an AI response is like a waiter bringing your courses as they're ready, rather than waiting until every dish is done and bringing them all at once. You start eating (reading) sooner, even though the full meal takes the same time to prepare.

Guardrails on an AI are like childproof caps on medicine bottles. The contents are useful but potentially dangerous if misused. The caps don't change the medicine — they add a layer of friction that prevents the most common accidental misuses.

A machine learning algorithm learns like a child sorting shapes. At first it guesses randomly, but each mistake provides feedback. Over thousands of attempts, it builds internal rules: 'round things go in the round hole.' It never reads the instructions — it discovers the rules through trial and error.

Overfitting is like memorizing a textbook word-for-word instead of understanding the concepts. You'll ace any question pulled directly from the book, but you'll bomb anything that's phrased differently or requires applying the knowledge to a new situation.

Frontend is the stage of a theater — lights, costumes, set design, what the audience sees. Backend is everything backstage — scripts, cue sheets, pulleys, and the crew making it all work. A great show needs both, but the audience only sees the stage.

An HTTP request-response cycle is like a walkie-talkie exchange. Your browser says 'over' (sends a request), the server processes it and replies 'over' (sends a response). Each exchange is independent — the server doesn't remember previous conversations unless you use cookies or sessions.

JSON is like a form that any office in the world can read. It uses a simple structure — labels and values, lists and groups — that every programming language understands. It's not the only data format, but it's the lingua franca of web APIs.

REST is like a vending machine with fixed buttons — press B3 and you get exactly what's behind B3, nothing more, nothing less. GraphQL is like a deli counter — you tell them exactly what you want on your sandwich and they build it to order.

OAuth is like a valet parking ticket. You hand over limited access (the valet can drive your car but can't open the trunk) without giving away your actual car key. The app gets a token that lets it do specific things on your behalf without knowing your password.

Cookies are like a stamp on your hand at a club. Each time you step out and come back, the bouncer checks the stamp instead of re-checking your ID. The stamp (cookie) is on your hand (browser); the guest list (session) is at the door (server).

CORS is like a building's visitor policy. Your office (origin) can access its own files freely, but to access files in another company's office (different origin), that company must have you on their approved visitor list. The browser enforces this policy automatically.

A WebSocket is like an open phone line between your browser and the server. Instead of hanging up after every question (HTTP), both sides keep the line open and can speak whenever they have something to say — perfect for chat apps, live scores, or real-time collaboration.

A webhook is like a doorbell. Instead of constantly checking if someone's at the door (polling), you install a doorbell (webhook) and go about your day. When someone arrives, they press it and you're notified immediately.

Environment variables are like a safe behind a painting in a manager's office. The code says 'get the API key from the safe' but never writes the actual key in any document. Different offices (environments) have different safes with different contents.

Localhost is like testing a dish in your own kitchen before serving it at a restaurant. Port numbers are burners on the stove — your web app runs on burner 3000, your database on burner 5432. Each service gets its own burner so they don't interfere.

HTML is the blueprint of a house — where the rooms, doors, and windows go. CSS is the interior design — paint colors, furniture style, lighting. JavaScript is the electrical and plumbing — the stuff that makes switches flip, faucets run, and the garage door open when you press a button.

The DOM is like a puppet version of your HTML. The browser reads your HTML file and builds a live puppet that JavaScript can manipulate — moving arms, changing clothes, adding hats — all without rewriting the original script.

Local storage is like writing a note in permanent marker on a whiteboard — it stays until someone deliberately erases it. Session storage is like writing in dry-erase marker — it disappears when you clean the board (close the tab).

Query parameters in a URL are like special instructions on a coffee order. The URL `coffee.com/order` says 'I want coffee.' Adding `?size=large&milk=oat` says 'I want a large oat milk coffee.' The barista (server) reads the slip and customizes accordingly.

Responsive design is like a newspaper layout that rearranges itself for different page sizes. A broadsheet shows articles in 6 columns; a tabloid reformats to 3; a mobile screen stacks them in 1. Same content, different arrangement depending on the container.

Server-side rendering is like a restaurant that plates your meal in the kitchen and brings it ready to eat. Client-side rendering ships you the raw ingredients and a recipe — your browser assembles the page. SSR is faster to first bite; CSR gives you more control over the cooking.

CRUD is the four verbs of any data app: Create (write a new Post-it), Read (look at the Post-it), Update (cross out a line and write a new one), Delete (crumple it up and toss it). Every to-do app, social feed, and admin panel is just CRUD in a nicer outfit.

HTTP status codes are like a traffic light system for the web. 2xx is green (all good), 3xx is a detour sign (redirected), 4xx is a 'road closed' sign (you made a wrong turn), and 5xx is an engine failure (the server broke down).

Routing is like a switchboard operator from the 1950s. A request comes in for `/products/shoes`, and the router connects it to the right handler — 'I'll patch you through to the shoes department.' Without routing, every URL would reach the same dead end.

HTTPS is like sending a letter in a locked box instead of a postcard. Anyone can read a postcard (HTTP) in transit. With HTTPS, you and the website exchange a unique padlock (SSL certificate) so only you two can open the box. The lock icon in your browser confirms the box is sealed.

Middleware is like the security checkpoint before you reach the gate at an airport. Your request passes through it on the way in (checking tickets, scanning bags) and sometimes on the way out. It doesn't fly the plane — it processes every passenger before they board.

A DDoS attack is like a flash mob blocking the entrance to a store. Nobody's stealing anything — they're just standing in the doorway so real customers can't get in. The store is fine inside; it's the entrance that's jammed.

The internet works like a relay race for data. Your message is split into packets, each handed off from router to router (like relay runners), each choosing the fastest available path. They may arrive out of order but get reassembled at the finish line.

Public-key cryptography is like a suggestion box. Anyone can slip a note through the slot (encrypt with the public key), but only the person with the key to the box can read the notes (decrypt with the private key). The slot is public; the key is private.

Net neutrality is like the postal service treating all letters equally regardless of who sent them. Without it, the post office could deliver Amazon's letters overnight while holding your local shop's letters for a week — unless they pay extra for priority.

Download vs upload speed is like a funnel — wide end down. ISPs make the pipe fatter for incoming data because most people consume (stream, browse) far more than they produce (upload files, video call). The asymmetry matches typical usage patterns.

A network backdoor is like a hidden door in a bank vault that bypasses the main lock. It might have been built for maintenance, but if a thief discovers it, they walk right past all the security. That's why backdoors are considered critical vulnerabilities.

The OS manages memory like an air traffic controller manages runways. Each program requests a landing slot (memory allocation). The controller tracks which slots are occupied, prevents collisions, and clears slots when planes depart (programs close).

concept: RAM management (free RAM is wasted RAM)
analogy: Unused RAM is like empty shelves in a busy kitchen. A smart chef keeps frequently used ingredients on the shelves (cached in RAM) rather than walking to the pantry (disk) each time. Empty shelves aren't 'saving space' — they're wasting speed.

Bandwidth is how many lanes a highway has. Latency is how long the highway is. A 10-lane highway (high bandwidth) doesn't help if it stretches 1,000 miles (high latency). Video calls need low latency (short road); large downloads need high bandwidth (many lanes).

Latency is how long it takes one person to get through a checkout line. Throughput is how many people get through per hour. Self-checkout might have lower throughput than a fast cashier, but if the lines are shorter, each person's latency is lower.

A CDN is like having photocopies of a popular library book placed in every branch across the city. Instead of everyone driving to the central library (origin server), you pick up the copy from your nearest branch. Same content, much shorter trip.

Logging is keeping a ship's logbook — recording events as they happen. Observability is having a full dashboard with radar, GPS, engine gauges, and weather feeds. Logs tell you what happened after the fact; observability lets you see the ship's health in real time.

CI/CD is like a test kitchen that automatically quality-checks every new recipe before it goes on the menu. A chef submits a dish (pushes code), the kitchen runs taste tests (automated tests), and if everything passes, it goes straight to diners (production). If a test fails, the dish is sent back.

Serverless is like hiring a temp worker for exactly one task. They show up, do the job, and leave — you only pay for the minutes they worked. You don't rent an office for them or worry about their benefits. The cloud provider handles all that.

Load balancing is like a host at a busy restaurant who seats new arrivals at the least crowded section. No single waiter gets overwhelmed, and customers get served faster because the work is spread evenly across all available staff.

Rate limiting is like a bouncer enforcing a 'one drink per 15 minutes' rule. You can keep ordering, but if you go too fast, the bouncer makes you wait. It protects the bar (server) from getting overwhelmed and ensures everyone gets served.

A cache is like keeping your most-used phone numbers memorized. You could look them up in your contacts every time, but for the ones you call daily, remembering them saves the lookup time. The tradeoff: your memory might be outdated if someone changes their number.

A database index is like alphabetical tabs in a filing cabinet. Without tabs, you'd flip through every folder to find 'Smith.' With tabs, you jump straight to the 'S' section. It costs a bit of extra space (the tabs themselves) but saves enormous search time.

SQL is like an Excel spreadsheet with strict column rules — every row must have the same fields. NoSQL is like a box of index cards — each card can have different information, making it flexible for varied data, but harder to run structured queries across all cards.

A database migration is like changing the plumbing in a house while people are still showering. You can't just rip out the pipes — you have to carefully reroute water, swap sections one at a time, and make sure nothing leaks during the transition.

Storage tiers are like how you organize things by how often you need them. Keys go in your pocket (register — instant). Today's to-do list goes on your desk (cache). This month's files go in a desk drawer (RAM). Tax records from 2019 go in the attic (disk). Each level trades access speed for capacity.

Version control is like Google Docs' version history for your entire project. Every change is saved with a timestamp and author. You can rewind to any point, see who changed what, and work on different versions simultaneously without losing anything.

A commit is like taking a polaroid of your project. The photo captures everything at that exact moment. You can keep working and take more photos, and if anything goes wrong, you can always go back and look at (or restore from) any previous photo.

The staging area is like a shopping cart before checkout. You browse the store (make changes), add specific items to the cart (stage files), review what's in the cart, and then checkout (commit). You don't have to buy everything you touched — just what's in the cart.

Git branching is like making a photocopy of a document to try edits. You scribble all over the copy (branch) without touching the original (main). If the edits work out, you transfer them to the original. If not, you toss the copy — the original is untouched.

A pull request is like submitting an article to an editor. You've written your piece (branch), and you're asking the editor (team) to review it before it gets published (merged into main). They might approve it, suggest changes, or send it back for a rewrite.

Git stash is like bookmarking your place in a book and putting it on the shelf. You need to do something else (switch branches), but you don't want to lose your spot. When you come back, you pull the bookmark out and resume exactly where you left off.

A merge conflict is like two architects who both redesigned the same room without telling each other. One made it a bedroom, the other a study. Git can't pick for you — it marks the conflicting area and asks you to decide which design to keep (or combine both).

Technical debt is like using duct tape to fix a leaky pipe. It works right now, but you know you'll have to do a proper repair eventually. The longer you leave it, the more duct tape piles up and the harder the real fix becomes.

Refactoring is like reorganizing your garage. You're not buying new tools or throwing old ones away — you're arranging everything so you can find what you need faster. The garage holds the same stuff; it's just better organized.

Open source licenses are like rules for a community garden plot. Some say 'take what you want, just mention where it came from.' Others say 'take what you want, but if you grow something new from these seeds, your garden must also be open to everyone.'

Recursion is like looking up a word in the dictionary and finding the definition uses another word you don't know — so you look that up too. Eventually you hit a word you understand (the base case) and work your way back through all the definitions.

Encryption is like writing a letter in a secret code that only you and your friend know. Anyone who intercepts the letter sees gibberish. The 'key' is the codebook that translates gibberish back into the original message.

Docker containers are like shipping containers on a cargo ship. It doesn't matter what's inside each container — electronics, bananas, furniture — the ship carries them all the same way. Similarly, Docker packages any app so it runs identically on any machine.

File compression is like abbreviating words in a text message. 'Because' becomes 'bc', 'tomorrow' becomes 'tmrw.' The message is smaller, but the receiver knows how to expand the abbreviations back. ZIP files do this with patterns in data.

Lossy compression is like retelling a story from memory. You keep the main plot points but lose the exact wording and minor details. Each retelling loses a bit more. The story still makes sense, but it's not identical to the original.

Dividing by zero crashes a program like asking 'how many groups of zero can you pull from ten?' You'd pull forever and never make progress. The computer recognizes this infinite loop and raises an error rather than spinning endlessly.

Logarithmic time is like the '20 questions' game. Each yes-or-no question cuts the remaining possibilities in half. Even with a million options, you need only about 20 questions. That's why binary search is so fast — each step halves the problem.

Programming in assembly is like writing a letter one character at a time on a typewriter while someone dictates. You control every single keystroke, which gives you total precision but takes forever compared to just speaking naturally (high-level languages).

Programmers think like architects designing around constraints. 'This wall is load-bearing, so we can't remove it, but we can add a beam.' They see problems as systems of dependencies and look for the smallest change that achieves the goal without breaking everything else.

A package manager is like an app store for code libraries. Instead of building every feature from scratch, you search for a pre-built solution, install it with one command, and the package manager handles updates and compatibility checks.

Dependency conflicts are like a dinner party where one guest is vegan and another insists on steak for every course. Both need to be satisfied, but their requirements clash. The resolution is finding a menu (version) that works for everyone.

A CPU is like a Swiss Army knife — one tool that does many different tasks well. A GPU is like a box of 1,000 identical screwdrivers — each can only do one thing, but when you need to drive 1,000 screws at once (AI calculations), the box wins by sheer parallelism.

CPU cores are like chefs in a kitchen. More chefs means more dishes prepared simultaneously. Threads are like each chef alternating between two recipes while one is in the oven — keeping busy during downtime. 8 cores with 16 threads means 8 chefs each juggling 2 dishes.

Clock speed is like the RPM of an engine. Higher RPM means the engine cycles faster, but it doesn't tell you how much work each cycle does. A sports car at 6,000 RPM and a truck at 6,000 RPM perform very differently — architecture matters as much as speed.

An SSD is like a light switch — flip it and the room is lit instantly. An HDD is like a record player — the needle has to physically travel to the right groove. That mechanical seek time is why HDDs feel sluggish for everyday tasks, even though they store tons of data cheaply.

VRAM is like the counter space in a kitchen. You can own all the ingredients in the world (stored on disk), but you can only actively cook with what fits on the counter (VRAM). A bigger AI model needs more counter space — run out, and you can't cook the recipe at all.

Binary is the simplest possible alphabet — just two letters: 0 and 1. A bit is one letter. A byte is an 8-letter word. With 8 bits you can spell 256 different 'words,' which is enough to represent any character you'd type. Everything your computer does is sentences written in this two-letter alphabet.

ARM vs x86 is like metric vs imperial. Both measure the same things, but the systems are incompatible. Software built for one won't run on the other without conversion. ARM (phones, Apple Silicon) prioritizes efficiency; x86 (Intel/AMD) prioritizes raw power.

Thermal throttling is like a car's rev limiter kicking in. The engine (CPU) could go faster, but the temperature is in the danger zone, so the system automatically caps performance to prevent damage. Better cooling raises the ceiling before throttling kicks in.

Storage tiers work like a chef's workspace. Spices on the counter (registers) — grab instantly. Prepped ingredients on the cutting board (cache) — reach over. Full pantry across the kitchen (RAM) — walk there. Bulk warehouse (disk) — drive to it. Speed decreases, capacity increases.

Computer overheating is like an engine running without oil. Every calculation generates friction (heat), and the cooling system is the oil that carries it away. Block the vents or push the CPU too hard, and heat builds up until the system protects itself by slowing down or shutting off.

Overclocking is like tuning a guitar string tighter to get a higher note. It works up to a point, but too tight and the string snaps. The CPU runs faster than its rated speed, generating more heat and requiring better cooling to stay stable.

A flash drive is like a wallet — small, portable, holds a few essentials. An external hard drive is like a suitcase — heavier, but carries your entire wardrobe. Flash drives sacrifice capacity for convenience; hard drives sacrifice portability for volume.

Growing hard drive capacity is like printing a newspaper in smaller and smaller font. Same size paper, more words per page. Each generation of drives writes data tracks more tightly, fitting more information in the same physical platter space.

Upscaling a small image is like inflating a small balloon with a drawing on it. The drawing stretches and gets blurry because you're adding rubber (pixels) with no new ink (data). AI upscaling tries to guess what ink should go in the new rubber, but it's still an educated guess.

Downscaling an image is like summarizing a book into a paragraph. You keep the main ideas (overall shapes and colors) but lose the fine details (individual sentences). The summary is much smaller but still conveys the gist.

Pixels are like pointillism — thousands of colored dots that form an image up close but get blocky when enlarged. Vectors are like geometric blueprints — 'draw a circle here, a line there' — that stay perfectly sharp at any size because they're math, not dots.

C# versions are like smartphone OS updates. Each new version adds features and improvements, but apps built for older versions still run. You don't need the latest version to get started — but newer versions make common tasks easier.

A DAC (digital-to-analog converter) is like a player piano reading a punch roll. The digital file is the roll with precise hole patterns (samples). The DAC reads each hole and presses the corresponding key, recreating the original music as a continuous sound wave.

E-ink refreshing is like rearranging magnets on a fridge. Each pixel is a capsule of tiny black and white particles that an electric charge pushes up or down. Rearranging all of them at once causes a brief flash — that flicker you see on every page turn.

Sending a ZIP instead of a folder is like mailing one package instead of 50 letters. The post office handles one delivery, the contents stay together, and nothing gets lost. The recipient opens one box instead of checking the mailbox 50 times.

ISP peering disputes are like two neighboring countries arguing over who maintains the bridge between them. If neither invests, the bridge gets congested, and the people (data packets) trying to cross suffer the slowdown.

concept: Inertial reference frames (jumping in a plane)
analogy: Jumping in a plane and landing in the same spot is like tossing a ball inside a moving car. The ball shares the car's speed, so from your perspective inside, it goes straight up and down. From outside, the ball traces an arc — same physics, different viewpoint.

An LCD pixel is like a sandwich: backlight (bread), liquid crystal layer (filling), and color filter (top bread). The crystal twists to let more or less light through, and the filter tints it red, green, or blue. Thousands of these tiny sandwiches form your screen image.

Your phone briefly showing Wi-Fi after the router dies is like a GPS showing your location for a few seconds inside a tunnel. The device displays its last known state until it actively discovers the connection is gone.

Wi-Fi traveling through walls is like shouting through a closed door. A thin door barely muffles your voice (drywall). A thick steel door blocks almost everything (metal walls). The signal doesn't stop — it just gets weaker with each obstacle.

Wireless data transmission is like blinking a flashlight in Morse code, but billions of times per second and with radio waves instead of visible light. The receiver watches the flashes and decodes the pattern back into data.
